title: Embedded Linux: Yocto/Buildroot, Device Tree & SBCs

lessons.title: What Is Embedded Linux?
lessons.content:
Here is a surprising fact: the same Linux that runs giant web servers also runs inside tiny everyday gadgets. Your Wi-Fi router, your smart TV, the screen in many cars, and a small hobbyist computer like a **Raspberry Pi** (a credit-card-sized computer) are very often running Linux. When Linux runs on a small device like that, we call it **embedded Linux** - "embedded" just means "built into a device" rather than sitting on a desk as a general computer.

So what is different about it? On a normal PC you install a big, general-purpose Linux **distribution** (a "distro" is a ready-made Linux bundle, like Ubuntu) that can do almost anything. A small device doesn't need almost anything - it needs to do one job well. So instead of installing a giant distro, engineers build a **small, custom Linux image** that contains only the pieces that one device needs.

An **image** here means the complete, ready-to-run system packed into a single file that gets loaded onto the device. Think of it like the difference between a fully stocked kitchen (a desktop distro - every tool, just in case) and a small travel kit with only the few utensils one recipe needs (an embedded image).

Why go to that trouble? Small devices have very little storage and memory, so a smaller image fits and runs better. A custom image also starts faster and has fewer moving parts to break.

Recap: embedded Linux is regular Linux, shrunk down and customized into a small image tailored to a single device - the Linux hiding inside routers, TVs, cars, and a Raspberry Pi.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what does "embedded Linux" mean?
choices: A version of Linux that can only run on supercomputers
Linux that is built into a device (like a router or TV) as a small, custom system rather than a general desktop install
A programming language used to write Linux
A type of Wi-Fi network created by Linux machines
items.explanation: "Embedded" means built into a device to do a specific job. Embedded Linux is the same Linux kernel, packaged as a small custom image for one device, rather than a big general-purpose desktop distribution.

items.prompt: Which of these everyday products is LEAST likely to be quietly running embedded Linux inside it?
choices: A home Wi-Fi router
A smart TV
A paper notebook
A Raspberry Pi
items.explanation: Routers, smart TVs, and the Raspberry Pi are all small computers that commonly run Linux. A paper notebook has no computer in it at all, so there is no software running inside it.

items.prompt: Why do engineers build a small, custom Linux image for a device instead of installing a full desktop distribution like Ubuntu?
choices: Because desktop Linux is illegal to use on small devices
Because small devices have little storage and memory, and only need the few parts required for their one job
Because custom images cannot connect to the internet
Because a Raspberry Pi cannot run any version of Ubuntu
items.explanation: Small devices are tight on storage and memory and do one focused job. A trimmed, custom image fits the hardware, starts faster, and has fewer parts that could fail. A full desktop distro would be bloated and wasteful here.

items.prompt: A Linux "distribution" (distro) such as Ubuntu or Fedora is best described as:
choices: A single program for editing photos
A ready-made bundle of the Linux kernel plus many programs, packaged for general use
A piece of hardware you plug into a device
The cable used to connect a board to a PC
items.explanation: A distribution bundles the Linux kernel together with a large collection of programs and tools into a ready-to-install package for general-purpose computers. Embedded projects usually skip the big distro and build a slim custom image instead.

items.prompt: In embedded Linux, what does an "image" refer to?
choices: A photograph stored on the device
The complete, ready-to-run system packed into a single file that is loaded onto the device
The device's screen output
A diagram of the circuit board
items.explanation: An image is the whole system (kernel plus the chosen programs and files) assembled into one file that gets written onto the device's storage. It is not a picture; it is the packaged software the device boots.

items.prompt: The lesson compares a desktop distro to a fully stocked kitchen and an embedded image to a small travel kit. What idea is that analogy teaching?
choices: Embedded devices cannot cook or do useful work
An embedded image includes only the few parts the device actually needs, while a desktop distro includes nearly everything just in case
Desktop Linux is always better than embedded Linux
Travel kits are more expensive than kitchens
items.explanation: The travel kit holds only what one recipe needs, just as an embedded image holds only what one device needs. The stocked kitchen, like a desktop distro, carries far more than any single task requires.

items.prompt: Is the Linux inside a Wi-Fi router fundamentally a different operating system from the Linux on a server?
choices: Yes, the router uses a completely unrelated operating system
No, it is the same Linux underneath, just shrunk down and customized into a small image for that device
Yes, embedded Linux is not really Linux at all
No, but only because routers are not real computers
items.explanation: It is the same Linux at its core. The difference is packaging: the router runs a small, tailored image with only what it needs, whereas a server runs a large general-purpose install.

items.prompt: Which is a real benefit of shipping a smaller, custom Linux image on a device?
choices: It guarantees the device can never be hacked
It fits the device's limited storage and memory, and tends to start faster with fewer parts to break
It makes the device run a completely different kernel
It removes the need for the device to have any storage at all
items.explanation: A trimmed image fits constrained hardware, boots faster, and has a smaller "surface" of components that could fail. It does not magically guarantee security, and the device still needs storage to hold the image.

items.prompt: Look at the diagram. What is the single clearest difference it shows between a desktop distro and an embedded image?
choices: The desktop distro has no kernel
The embedded image contains only the few parts the device needs, while the desktop distro carries thousands of general-purpose programs
The embedded image is for big servers and the distro is for tiny boards
They are identical in every way
items.explanation: The diagram contrasts a large, do-everything desktop distro with a slim embedded image tailored to one device. Both use the same Linux underneath; the embedded side simply includes only what that one device needs.

lessons.title: How a Board Boots: the ARM Boot Chain
lessons.content:
When you press power on a small Linux board, getting to a working system is a **relay race**: several small programs run one after another, each waking up the next and handing over the baton. This sequence is called the **boot chain**. Most of these boards use **ARM** chips - a family of processors common in phones and small devices - so people call it the ARM boot chain.

Here are the stages, in order:

1. **Boot ROM.** A tiny piece of code permanently baked into the chip at the factory. It runs first, the instant power arrives. Its only job is to find and load the next stage.
2. **U-Boot.** This is the **bootloader** - a small program whose job is to find the Linux kernel, load it into memory, and start it. (You may meet a tiny helper before it, often called the SPL, whose only task is to switch on the memory chips so U-Boot has room to run; for now, just picture "a loader that starts the kernel.")
3. **Kernel + device tree.** The Linux **kernel** is the core of Linux - the part that actually controls the hardware. Alongside it, U-Boot hands over a small file called the **device tree** that lists what hardware is on this board (the next lesson explains this in depth).
4. **init.** Once Linux is running, it starts the very first program, called **init**. init launches the services the device needs and finally gives you a **login** prompt.

How is this different from a normal PC? A PC's first stage is built-in firmware called **BIOS/UEFI**, not a boot ROM plus U-Boot. And a PC can automatically detect its own hardware, so it needs no device tree. A small board cannot auto-detect its parts - which is exactly why the device tree exists.

Recap: power on, then Boot ROM, then U-Boot loads the kernel, the kernel reads the device tree and starts init, and you reach a login. Each runner hands the baton to the next.

items.prompt: What is the "boot chain" of a Linux board?
choices: A physical chain that locks the board to a desk
The ordered sequence of small programs that each start the next, from power-on up to a running system
The power cable that supplies the board
A list of users allowed to log in
items.explanation: The boot chain is the relay of programs that run in order after power-on - boot ROM, then the bootloader, then the kernel, then init - each handing off to the next until you have a usable system.

items.prompt: What is the correct order of the ARM boot chain described in the lesson?
choices: Kernel, then U-Boot, then boot ROM, then init
Boot ROM, then U-Boot, then kernel + device tree, then init
init, then kernel, then boot ROM, then U-Boot
U-Boot, then boot ROM, then init, then kernel
items.explanation: Power arrives and the on-chip boot ROM runs first, it loads U-Boot, U-Boot loads the kernel (with its device tree), and the kernel starts init. Each stage hands off to the next in that order.

items.prompt: What runs first the instant the board gets power, and where does it live?
choices: U-Boot, stored on the SD card
The boot ROM, a tiny program baked into the chip itself at the factory
The Linux kernel, loaded from the internet
init, the first user program
items.explanation: The boot ROM is permanent code inside the chip. Because it is always there and always runs first, it can reliably start the boot process and load the next stage.

items.prompt: What is the main job of U-Boot, the bootloader?
choices: To edit photos on the device
To find the Linux kernel, load it into memory, and start it
To permanently store all the user's files
To detect the hardware so Linux never needs a device tree
items.explanation: A bootloader's purpose is to locate the kernel, place it in memory, and hand control to it. U-Boot does exactly that, and also passes along the device tree so the kernel knows what hardware to expect.

items.prompt: In one sentence, what is the Linux "kernel"?
choices: The outer plastic case of the device
The core of Linux - the part that actually controls the hardware and runs programs
A single app for browsing the web
The login screen shown to the user
items.explanation: The kernel is the central piece of Linux that manages the hardware and lets programs run. The bootloader's whole purpose is to get this kernel loaded and started.

items.prompt: After Linux starts, what does "init" do?
choices: It powers the board off
It is the first program Linux runs; it starts the device's services and brings up a login prompt
It loads the kernel into memory
It is the factory code baked into the chip
items.explanation: init is the first program the kernel launches once it is running. It starts the needed background services and ends at a login prompt - the visible sign that boot finished.

items.prompt: How does a board's boot start differently from a normal PC's?
choices: A PC has no operating system at all
A PC's first stage is built-in firmware called BIOS/UEFI, instead of a boot ROM plus U-Boot
A PC never loads a kernel
A board cannot be powered on
items.explanation: On a PC, the first code to run is firmware (BIOS or UEFI) built into the motherboard. A small board instead starts from an on-chip boot ROM that chains to U-Boot. Both ultimately load a Linux kernel.

items.prompt: Why does a small board need a device tree while a normal PC does not?
choices: A PC has no hardware to describe
A PC can automatically detect its own hardware, but a small board cannot, so the board needs a file describing its parts
A device tree is only used for drawing graphics
Boards are faster than PCs and skip the step
items.explanation: PCs use standards that let them discover attached hardware on their own. A small custom board's parts are silent and cannot announce themselves, so a device tree file tells the kernel what is present.

items.prompt: The lesson calls booting a "relay race." What is that analogy highlighting?
choices: That booting is a competition between devices
That several small programs run one after another, each starting (handing the baton to) the next
That the kernel runs all the stages at the same time
That booting only works if the device is moving
items.explanation: Like runners passing a baton, each boot stage does its part and then hands control to the next stage. No single program does everything; they cooperate in sequence.

items.prompt: In the boot-chain diagram, which handoff is shown happening just before you reach a usable login?
choices: Boot ROM hands directly to init, skipping everything
The kernel (with the device tree) starts init, which then brings up the login
init starts U-Boot
The login starts the boot ROM
items.explanation: The diagram shows the chain ending with the kernel handing off to init, which launches services and presents the login. The boot ROM and U-Boot are earlier stages, already finished by then.

lessons.title: The Device Tree: Telling Linux What Hardware Exists

items.prompt: Why does embedded Linux need a device tree at all?
choices: Because Linux refuses to boot without drawing a tree on screen
Because many parts on a small board are silent and cannot announce themselves, so the kernel needs a file describing what hardware is present
Because the device tree replaces the need for a kernel
Because it makes the device boot faster than a PC
items.explanation: This is the core idea: a small board's parts (like an I2C sensor or a wired LED) can't tell the kernel they exist. The device tree is the text file that describes them so the kernel knows what is there and can drive it.

items.prompt: In plain words, what IS a device tree?
choices: A physical tree-shaped circuit on the board
A text file that lists the hardware on the board and tells the kernel what each part is and where it is
A live video feed of the board
A list of websites the device may visit
items.explanation: A device tree is a description of the board's hardware in a text file - essentially a parts list handed to the kernel at boot. It is not a physical object or a graphic.

items.prompt: Why can a USB stick on a PC be recognized automatically, while a sensor wired to a small board often cannot?
choices: USB sticks contain their own copy of Linux
USB devices can announce their name and type, but a simple wired part is silent and has no way to introduce itself
The board is broken if a sensor is not detected
PCs do not actually detect USB devices either
items.explanation: USB has a built-in way for a device to report what it is. A bare sensor or LED wired to the chip has no such mechanism, so something must describe it - that something is the device tree.

items.prompt: What does the "compatible" line in a device-tree entry do?
choices: It sets the screen brightness
It is a name tag identifying the kind of part, which Linux uses to pick the matching driver
It lists the price of the part
It deletes the part from the system
items.explanation: The compatible string names the part's type. The kernel matches that name to a driver; on a match, the right driver loads and operates the part. It is the link between the hardware description and the software.

items.prompt: In this context, what is a "driver"?
choices: The person who installed the board
A small piece of software that knows how to operate a specific piece of hardware
The cable connecting the board to a PC
The main login screen
items.explanation: A driver is the software that knows the details of one kind of hardware and lets the rest of the system use it. The device tree's compatible name tells Linux which driver to load for each part.

items.prompt: You added a sensor to the device tree but after boot it does nothing - no error, just silence. The driver for it is present. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The board ran out of electricity
The compatible name has a typo, so Linux finds no matching driver and the part quietly does nothing
The kernel is too new to boot
The login prompt is disabled
items.explanation: This is the classic device-tree bug. If the compatible name does not exactly match what the driver expects, no driver binds and the part stays silent. The fix is to correct the text, rebuild the device tree, and load it again.

items.prompt: What does the ".dts" file ending tell you about the device tree?
choices: It is already in the final binary form the kernel reads
It is the human-readable source text you can open and edit
It is a damaged file that cannot be used
It is a photograph of the board
items.explanation: ".dts" means device tree source - the editable text version. You change the .dts, then rebuild it into the form the kernel actually consumes.

items.prompt: What is a device-tree "overlay" useful for?
choices: Making the screen background transparent
Adding an entry for an optional add-on part without rewriting the whole device tree
Permanently deleting the kernel
Encrypting the user's files
items.explanation: An overlay is a small patch that adds or enables a part on top of the base device tree. It is handy for optional hardware (like a sensor you sometimes attach) because you don't have to rebuild the entire list.

items.prompt: After editing the device tree source to fix a part, what must you do for the change to take effect?
choices: Nothing; the kernel re-reads the source text live while running
Rebuild the device tree from the corrected source and load the new version on the board
Buy a new board
Delete the driver entirely
items.explanation: The kernel reads the built device tree at boot, not the source text live. So after editing the .dts you must rebuild it and deploy the new version, then reboot, for the change to apply.

items.prompt: In the device-tree diagram, what links a silent part on the board to the right driver in Linux?
choices: Its color
Its "compatible" name, which Linux matches to a driver
The length of the wires
The board's serial number
items.explanation: The diagram shows each part described by an entry whose compatible name is the matching key. Linux reads that name and loads the corresponding driver; a typo in the name breaks the match.

lessons.title: Build Tools: Buildroot vs Yocto (and the BSP)
lessons.content:
We've said an embedded device runs a small, custom image. But who actually assembles that image? You don't hand-pick thousands of files yourself. Instead you use a **build system** - a tool that takes your choices and automatically **cross-compiles** and assembles everything into one finished image. ("Cross-compile" means build the software on your fast PC but produce programs that run on the board's different chip - covered in the earlier build sub-topic.)

First, a head start: a **BSP** (Board Support Package) is a ready-made bundle from the board's maker that gets that specific board to boot - it includes the bootloader, a kernel with the right drivers, and the board's device tree. You build your custom image on top of the BSP rather than from nothing.

There are two popular build systems, and the key is knowing which to reach for.

**Buildroot** is the simple one. You pick what you want from a menu of checkboxes, press build, and it produces one small image. It is quick to learn and quick to build, and it is ideal for a single, fixed device. Think of it as a friendly build-your-own kit. For most beginners and small projects, this is the right starting point.

**Yocto** (also called OpenEmbedded) is the industrial one. It is built from **recipes** (each recipe is a small instruction file describing how to build one component) and **layers** (stacks of recipes you can reuse and combine). It is far more powerful and flexible, and it scales to large product families maintained for years - but it is heavier, harder to learn, and slower to build. Think of it as a full factory production line.

**When to use which?** Choose Buildroot when you want something simple and fast for one fixed image. Choose Yocto when you need to scale across many products, reuse lots of components, and maintain them over a long lifetime.

Recap: a build system assembles your image for you; a BSP gives you a working starting point for the board; Buildroot is simple and great to start with, while Yocto is the heavier industrial choice for big, long-lived products.

items.prompt: What is a "build system" like Buildroot or Yocto for?
choices: It is the screen that displays the device's output
It is a tool that automatically assembles and cross-compiles all the pieces into one finished image
It is the chip inside the board
It is a website where you buy boards
items.explanation: A build system takes your choices and does the heavy lifting: fetching, cross-compiling, and packing everything into a single image you can put on the device. You don't assemble thousands of files by hand.

items.prompt: A small team needs a minimal, fixed Linux image for one Raspberry Pi product and wants to be productive quickly. Which build system fits best, and why?
choices: Yocto, because it is the only tool that can build any image
Buildroot, because its menu-based flow is simple and fast for a single, fixed image
Yocto, because Buildroot cannot build a kernel
Neither; you must assemble every file by hand
items.explanation: Buildroot's checkbox-menu approach gets a small fixed image built quickly and is easy to learn - ideal here. Yocto's extra power (recipes and layers) is overkill for one simple product and slower to get going.

items.prompt: A company is launching a whole family of products across many different boards, to be maintained and updated for years. Which build system is the better fit, and why?
choices: Buildroot, because long-lived products must avoid menus
Yocto, because its recipes and layers are built to reuse components and scale across many products over a long lifetime
Buildroot, because Yocto cannot support more than one board
Neither; large products cannot use a build system
items.explanation: Yocto is the industrial choice: its layered, recipe-based design is made for reusing components and maintaining many products over years. That power is worth its steeper learning curve when you operate at that scale.

items.prompt: What is a BSP (Board Support Package)?
choices: A snack provided with the board
A ready-made bundle from the board's maker (bootloader, a kernel with the right drivers, and the device tree) that gets that board to boot
A type of screw used to mount the board
A password that unlocks the device
items.explanation: A BSP is the vendor's starting bundle that makes a specific board boot. You build your custom image on top of it rather than starting from scratch.

items.prompt: In Yocto, what is a "recipe"?
choices: A cooking instruction unrelated to software
A small instruction file describing how to build one component of the image
The finished image itself
The board's serial number
items.explanation: A Yocto recipe is a build instruction for a single piece of software - where to get it and how to build it. Many recipes combine to produce the whole image.

items.prompt: What are Yocto "layers"?
choices: Physical layers of metal on the board
Stacks of recipes that can be reused and combined to build images
The login screens shown to different users
Levels of a video game
items.explanation: Layers group recipes so they can be reused and combined across projects and boards. This layering is a big part of why Yocto scales well to many products.

items.prompt: What does it mean that the build system "cross-compiles" the software?
choices: It builds the software on the board itself because the board is fastest
It builds on your fast PC but produces programs that run on the board's different chip
It refuses to build unless two PCs are connected
It translates the software into a different human language
items.explanation: Cross-compiling means doing the build work on a powerful host (your PC) while targeting the board's processor. The small board is usually too limited to build its own image.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the trade-off between Buildroot and Yocto?
choices: Buildroot is powerful but slow; Yocto is simple but weak
Buildroot is simple and fast but better for one fixed image; Yocto is powerful and scalable but heavier and harder to learn
They are identical, just with different names
Buildroot only works on PCs and Yocto only works on phones
items.explanation: The honest summary: Buildroot trades flexibility for simplicity and speed (great for small fixed projects), while Yocto trades ease-of-use for power and scale (great for large, long-lived product lines).

items.prompt: Do Buildroot and Yocto build software on the device itself?
choices: Yes, both run entirely on the small board
No, they run on your PC and cross-compile the image for the board
Yes, but only Yocto does
No, neither one builds anything; the board comes pre-built
items.explanation: Both build systems run on a capable host machine and cross-compile the result for the target board, because the board is typically too small and slow to build its own image.

items.prompt: The diagram pictures Buildroot as a basic build-your-own kit and Yocto as a full factory line. What point is it making?
choices: Buildroot cannot produce a working image
Buildroot is the simple, quick choice while Yocto is the powerful, industrial choice for large-scale, long-term work
Yocto is always the wrong tool
Both tools are toys, not real software
items.explanation: The kit-versus-factory image captures the trade-off: Buildroot is simple and fast for small jobs; Yocto is heavier but built for scale and long-term maintenance across many products.

lessons.title: The Root Filesystem, and Booting a Real Board
lessons.content:
Your image contains a **root filesystem** - the collection of files and folders Linux needs to run: its programs, settings, and the kernel's helpers. On embedded devices this is usually designed to be **tough**, because a device in the real world can lose power at any second (someone unplugs the router).

The common trick is a **read-only** root filesystem. "Read-only" means nothing can be changed or written to it while the device runs. Why is that good? If you pull the plug mid-write, a writable filesystem can get corrupted; a read-only one cannot, because nothing was being written. A popular read-only format is **squashfs**, which is also **compressed** (squeezed smaller to save precious storage).

But a device sometimes needs to save a little data - settings, logs. The solution is an **overlay**: a small writable layer stacked on top of the read-only base. The bulk stays safely read-only; only the few files that must change live in the writable overlay. Best of both worlds.

Now the payoff: putting your image on a real board. The board here is an **SBC** (Single-Board Computer - a whole computer on one small board, like a Raspberry Pi). The steps:

1. **Write the image to an SD card.** A built image is usually one .img file; a tool copies it onto the card. Be careful to pick the SD card and not your PC's own disk - choosing the wrong target can erase your computer.
2. **Put the card in the board and power it on.** The boot chain from the earlier lesson runs.
3. **Watch it over the serial console.** A small board often has no monitor or keyboard. A **serial console** is a simple text connection over a cable to your PC, so you can see the boot messages and reach the login prompt.

The most accessible SBC for learning is the **Raspberry Pi**; another well-known open-hardware board is the **BeagleBone**.

Recap: the root filesystem holds everything Linux needs; embedded devices keep it read-only (often compressed squashfs) with a small writable overlay for safety; then you write the image to an SD card, boot the SBC, and watch it come up over a serial console.

items.prompt: What is the "root filesystem" of an embedded Linux image?
choices: The roots of a plant growing near the board
The collection of files and folders Linux needs to run - its programs, settings, and helpers
The single most important user account
The metal base the board sits on
items.explanation: The root filesystem is the set of files Linux needs to operate: programs, configuration, and support files. It is part of the image you build and put on the device.

items.prompt: Why do many embedded devices use a read-only root filesystem?
choices: To make the device run faster than any PC
So that a sudden power loss cannot corrupt it, since nothing is being written to it
Because Linux cannot read writable filesystems
To prevent the device from ever booting
items.explanation: A device can be unplugged at any moment. A read-only filesystem has nothing being written, so an abrupt power cut can't corrupt it - exactly the robustness embedded products need.

items.prompt: What does "read-only" mean for a filesystem?
choices: Only the root user is allowed to look at it
Nothing can be changed or written to it while the device runs; it can only be read
It can only store text, never programs
It deletes itself after one read
items.explanation: Read-only means the contents are fixed during operation - they can be read but not modified. That is what makes it safe against corruption from interrupted writes.

items.prompt: What is squashfs, and why is it popular on embedded devices?
choices: A game pre-installed on Raspberry Pi
A compressed, read-only filesystem format that resists corruption and saves storage
A tool that erases SD cards
The cable used for the serial console
items.explanation: squashfs is read-only (so it resists corruption) and compressed (so it saves limited storage). Both qualities make it a natural fit for the base root filesystem on small devices.

items.prompt: If the base filesystem is read-only, how does the device still save small bits of data like settings or logs?
choices: It cannot save anything at all
An overlay - a small writable layer stacked on top of the read-only base - holds the few files that must change
It rewrites the kernel each time
It mails the data to the manufacturer
items.explanation: An overlay adds a thin writable layer over the protected read-only base. The bulk stays safe and unchangeable, while just the files that need updating live in the writable overlay.

items.prompt: What is an SBC?
choices: A Standard Battery Connector
A Single-Board Computer - a whole computer on one small board, like a Raspberry Pi
A Secure Boot Code
A Serial Bus Cable
items.explanation: SBC stands for Single-Board Computer: a complete computer built onto one compact board. The Raspberry Pi is the most common example for learning.

items.prompt: What is the critical precaution when writing your image file to an SD card?
choices: Make sure the SD card is brand new and empty of stickers
Be sure you pick the SD card as the target, not your PC's own disk, or you could erase your computer
Always unplug the monitor first
Rename the image file to end in .txt
items.explanation: Writing an image overwrites whatever target you choose. If you accidentally pick your computer's own disk instead of the SD card, you can wipe your machine - so double-check the target first.

items.prompt: What is a "serial console" and why is it useful for a small board?
choices: A games console that connects in series
A simple text connection over a cable to your PC, used to see boot messages and log in when the board has no monitor or keyboard
A second power supply for the board
A backup SD card slot
items.explanation: Small boards often have no screen or keyboard. A serial console gives you a plain-text window over a cable so you can watch it boot and reach the login prompt from your PC.

items.prompt: Which SBC does the lesson call the most accessible one for someone learning embedded Linux?
choices: A full desktop tower PC
The Raspberry Pi
A smart TV
A Wi-Fi router
items.explanation: The Raspberry Pi is widely available, inexpensive, and well-documented, making it the most beginner-friendly board to try building and booting your own image. The BeagleBone is another well-known open-hardware option.

items.prompt: What is the correct order to get your image running on a real board?
choices: Power on the board first, then build the image, then never use a card
Write the image to an SD card, put the card in the board and power it on, then watch it boot over the serial console
Email the image to the board, then watch a monitor
Plug the serial cable into the SD card directly
items.explanation: First write the built image onto the SD card, then insert it and power the board so the boot chain runs, then observe the boot and log in over the serial console - especially when there's no monitor attached.

items.prompt: The diagram traces image to running board. What is the last stage it shows, where you actually see the board boot?
choices: The image file on the PC
The serial console - a text window on your PC showing boot messages and the login
The SD card sitting on a desk
The build system menu
items.explanation: The diagram ends at the serial console: after the image is written to a card and the board boots, the serial console is your text window into the running board when there's no monitor or keyboard.
